data.title: Financial Modeling Of IonQ - A Quantum Leader Going Through Hypergrowth
data.description:
IonQ, Inc (IONQ) reported 2025 earnings last month, and the results came in eerily close to the projections in my previous article written in September of last year.

In that article, I pieced together all known acquisitions made by IonQ at the time, along with a rough estimate of organic growth, to come to a best guess of their 2025 and 2026 revenues.

In that same article, I also gave a brief background on the quantum industry as a whole, the strengths and weaknesses of the various quantum computing modalities, adjacent sectors such as quantum networking and sensing, and why I believe IonQ holds a pole position as the only full-stack quantum company providing end-to-end quantum solutions.

My view of IonQ's technological and market positions has not changed. With that in mind, I will focus solely on the financial aspects of the company in this update.

Review of 2025 Results

Back in September of last year, I estimated that IonQ would do $133M of revenue in 2025, excluding interest on their cash balance which I estimated to be $20M. The actual results came in very close at $130M revenue, although if we include actual cash interest of $56M, the total would be $185M which greatly trounced my estimate of $153M.

I under-estimated their interest on cash because of an additional capital raise in October that increased IonQ's total cash hoard to a gigantic $3.5B.

For 2026, I projected $267 of revenue (again excluding interest on cash) in the September article. IonQ's 2026 guidance came in at $225-245 last month. But given IonQ has a history of sandbagging and then beating the top-end of guidance, I am quite confident that they will meet or beat my previous estimate.

My Sep Estimate

Official Results
2025 Rev Actual ($M) 133 130
2025 Rev + Int Actual ($M) 153 185
2026 Rev Guidance ($M) 267 225-245

Let's talk a bit about organic growth. IonQ's CFO Inder Singh said this on the conference call:

Importantly, our 2025 results included nearly 80% year-on-year organic growth, and I expect this to be even healthier in 2026.

IonQ's 2024 revenue was only $43M. 80% organic growth would put it at $77M - a bit more than the $65M I estimated in my old article, which means I may have overestimated the acquired components of IonQ's revenue by $12M - not surprising as the number I got was patched together from multiple private market sources. Had IonQ only achieved organic revenue of $65M, that would still amount to organic growth of 51%.

Anyway you look at it, this pours cold water on the typical bear thesis that all of IonQ's growth was acquired and they have "no organic growth".

This company is too early in its hypergrowth phase for us to look at profitability. However, we should consider its cash burn to determine how much of a runway they have before they need to become profitable.

In 2025, IonQ used $16.4M of cash on CapEx and $283.2M on operating activities, for a total cash burn of $299.6M. Given the significant business changes and combinations that happened throughout the year, it's probably a good idea to sanity check this number with the Q4 cash burn. IonQ does not provide quarter-by-quarter cashflow, but if we use Q4 Adjusted EBITDA of -67.4M as proxy, their annualized cash burn would be $270M. To be conservative, I will use the larger number of $299.6M/year as their cash burn rate.

This gives them more than 11 years of runway based on year-end liquidity position of $3.3B.

Near-term Projection (2026/2027)

We have quite clear visibility into how IonQ will do in 2026 and even in 2027.

IonQ is expected to close 3 acquisitions this year. One is Skyloom, a developer of lightwave-optics communications technology that will enable satellite-to-satellite quantum networks. This was completed as of Jan 28.

Another deal was Seed Innovations - a quantum software engineering firm which closed on Jan 30.

IonQ issued about 5M shares for these 2 acquisitions which is roughly 1.4% share dilution. According to ZoomInfo, Skyloom's annual revenue was approximately $22M, and Seed Innovations was doing <$5M in revenue.

Both of these acquisitions closed in January and IonQ's earnings release was in February, so we have to assume that their 2026 guidance already incorporated revenues from these 2 acquisitions.

The 3rd and most significant deal is SkyWater (SKYT), a US-based semiconductor foundry with exposure to manufacturing quantum circuits. This deal turns IonQ into the world's first fully vertically integrated quantum platform company and greatly accelerates their quantum computing roadmap. It is widely expected to close by Q2 or Q3 of this year.

Let's first estimate where revenue will be without SkyWater.

In terms of organic growth, CFO's words quoted in the previous section indicated that 2026 will be "healthier" than 2025's 80% growth.

Conservatively assuming just 80% organic growth (i.e. disregarding IonQ's habit of lowballing) on 2025 revenue of $130M (rather than on 2025 annual run-rate inclusive of full year results of all the companies they bought in the year, which would give an even higher baseline of $200M+) gives us $234M in 2026.

Adding 11 months of revenue from Skyloom and Seed Innovations gives us approximately $259M without SkyWater - slightly above the top-end of their guidance of $245M, and in-line with my expectation that IonQ tend to set guidance low just to have it beat. Hitting this number would give them another year of 100% growth, continuing a powerful historic trend.

Now let's include SkyWater.

SkyWater was going through significant business transformation itself prior to the deal, having acquired Infineon's US Texas fab last year. Infineon is a German specialist semiconductor manufacturer who already has a prior relationship with IonQ, as they are the supplier of quantum circuits for Oxford Ionics, another IonQ company.

Post-transformation, SkyWater did revenue of $171M in Q4 2025 which translates to $684M annual run-rate.

SkyWater's 2026 revenue contribution to IonQ will largely depend on timing of the acquisition's completion - about $85.5M if it completes by Q2, or $43M if it completes by Q3, conservatively assume 0 growth.

Combined, I estimate IonQ will reach revenue of $302-345M in 2026, depending on timing of SkyWater acquisition.

In 2027, IonQ will have the full year revenue impact from all of its acquisitions. Without any growth at all, IonQ can simply "cruise" to a revenue of $945M ($234M baseline, $27M between Skyloom and Seed, and and $684M from SkyWater) - very close to a billion, 4 years ahead of ex-CEO Peter Chapman's 2030 target.

If we assume a continuation of 80% growth in IonQ's quantum business and 0 growth in the semiconductor fabrication business, IonQ's 2027 revenue will hit $1.15B. I expect actual results to come in somewhere between these 2 extremes.

Scenarios
Rev Projections Without SKYT Low-end with SKYT Upper-end with SKYT
2026 ($M) 259 302 345
2027 ($M) 259-466 945 1150

The SkyWater acquisition is structured as a cash-and-stock deal where each share of SKYT will be bought out with $15 of cash and 0.5265 IonQ shares (assuming IONQ stays below the collar floor of $37.99). With 48.6M shares of SKYT outstanding, this amounts to $729M cash and 25.6M shares of IONQ.

IonQ will also be assuming SkyWater's net debt of approximately 345M.

IONQ share count has risen to 366.6M after issuing 5M shares for Skyloom and Seed. SkyWater will further dilute the share count to 392.2M, which would give IonQ a market cap of close to $13B at current market price of around 33.

IonQ's net cash hoard is expected to decrease to $2.1B after SkyWater and 2 quarters of cash burn - reducing runway to 7 years, which is still ample.

Of course, we will have to re-evaluate everything if IonQ announces any further material acquisitions, but CEO Niccolo de Masi indicated a pause during a recent interview with Bloomberg, so perhaps we're entering a period of digestion and consolidation:

We've done a lot [of acquisitions] in 2025, and 2026 will be mostly powered by organic growth.

Long-term Projection (5 Years)

It's extremely challenging to project the long-term economics of any company going through rapid growth in an industry as dynamic as quantum. Nonetheless, we must attempt it as investors.

For this exercise, I'll try to answer the question of profitability - both when and how much. For that, I'll need to bring in a few more assumptions.

At the earnings call we learned that "remaining performance obligation" accelerated from $77M from end of 2024 to $370 at end of 2025. Intuitively, the acceleration makes sense as quantum computing is just starting to become commercially viable.

In fact, I expect a further "hockey-stick" acceleration in 2028/2029 as IonQ's quantum computers reach utility scale a full 2-3 years ahead of competitors, with >1000 logical qubits in production, and tape-out of their 200,000 physical qubit/8000 logical qubit system.

I'm baking in 100% growth in 2028 and 2029 before it starts tapering back down to 80% in 2030. For SkyWater, I'm conservatively assuming 0 growth, and I expect the that part of the business to weigh down overall growth rate of IonQ somewhat.

IonQ's gross margin was 40% in 2025, and SkyWater was 20% for the last 2 years. I expect these margins to be maintained.

IonQ has 686M of fixed costs excluding any cash interest income. SkyWater is already a roughly breakeven business as a standalone. It has 102M of annualized fixed costs post transformation.

IonQ may extinguish SkyWater debt with its warchest of cash, but if not, the interest income more than offsets any interests paid on the existing SkyWater debt. I will conservatively disregard the excess interests from the projection.

For 2026 & 2027 revenue numbers, I relied on previous section's analysis.

Putting it all together:

2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
IonQ Revenue 259 466 932 1,864 3,355
SKYT Revenue Contribution 324 684 684 684 684
Combined Revenue 583 1,150 1,616 2,548 4,039
Blended Growth % 97% 41% 58% 59%
Gross Margin 168 323 510 882 1,479
Fixed Costs 737 788 808 828 849
Pre-tax Earnings (569) (465) (298) 55 630

With the above assumptions, I expect IonQ to hit profitability by 2029. At the market cap of $13B, it is trading at 20.6x 2030 pre-tax earnings, which I feel is reasonable - even undervalued - for an industry leader going through transformative growth.

Risks

Needless to say, IonQ's valuation depends on it maintaining an outsized growth rate as an industry leader. And to do that, they must deliver on their ambitious technical roadmap.

They are set to deliver 256-qubit system based on the Oxford Ionics architecture this year. Investors should watch out for any indication of a delay - any major unexpected technical setback could make the rest of the roadmap questionable.

Another major risk factor is if IonQ makes any bad acquisitions that are material to the company. The acquisitions made so far mostly have good strategic fit, in my opinion, and the benefits outweighed the cost of dilution. However, the effects from one disastrous acquisition could take years to undo.

Takeaway

I was very accurate with my estimation of IonQ's 2025 results. In this article, I attempted to repeat that exercise for 2026, 2027 and beyond.

IonQ has consistently demonstrated high organic growth, and they have a habit of lowballing guidance and then beating the top-end.

Based on what is known about their guidance, latest acquisitions, and business prospects, I expect IonQ to reach over a billion in revenue by 2027, profitability by 2029, and become comfortably profitable by 2030.

Current valuation is very reasonable for investors who understand the potential of quantum computing, believe in IonQ's roadmap, and would like to take part in a public company going through startup-like hypergrowth.

data.title: What Happens After The S&P 500 Breaks Its 200-Day Moving Average
data.description:
While the war in the Middle East continues unabated, a new war of words broke out this week between President Trump and the Iranian officials with whom he says he is negotiating. This war is over markets. Trump wants lower oil prices (CL1:COM) and higher stock prices (SPY) for obvious reasons. Iran wants higher oil prices and lower stock prices to exact a punishing economic toll on the President in hopes that it will force him to retreat on their terms. Trump wants the conflict to end quickly, which he now defines as four to six weeks, while Iran wants to stretch it out as long as possible. The longer it lasts, the more economic pain for the U.S. economy.

This is why early Monday morning the President posted on his social media account that good and productive conversations with the Iranians were ongoing regarding ending the conflict. Whether that was true or not, stock prices soared and oil plunged more than $10, which was clearly the immediate objective. The Iranians countered in this war of words by denying any negotiations had occurred. Oil edged higher, and stocks gave back some of the gains the following day. Optimism in markets returned yesterday after news that the President’s 15-point plan to end the war was presented to Iranian officials through a Pakistani intermediary.

Yesterday’s optimism is waning again, with stock futures lower and oil higher, as the President’s proposal was dismissed and countered with five conditions that need to be met before Iran will agree to end the war. These include payment for war damages, mechanisms to ensure future attacks do not happen, a halt to all U.S. military operations, including in Israel and other Arab nations, and recognition of Iran’s sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. The good news is that these proposals do constitute a form of negotiation. The bad news is that neither side is likely to get the entirety of their wish list, and a resolution looks highly unlikely by the end of this week, which is the President’s extended deadline from the original 48-hours he gave Iran on Saturday evening.

The President’s jawboning has helped limit the downside in risk assets, which I’ll call the Trump put, because investors know that all it takes is a post to send stocks soaring and oil prices plunging. He can do it at any time. The problem is that this can be used only so many times before investors start to question the validity of the claims. This tactic may work a time or two more, but if the Iranians continue to assert false claims, then it will start to fall on deaf investors ears.

From an investment standpoint, the key variable here is time. The longer it takes to reach an agreement that ends the conflict and reopens the strait, the more damage that occurs to the economy, increasing the probability of an economic downturn later this year. This gives Iran leverage in the negotiations, as they continued to launch missile and drone strikes on Wednesday, despite assertions that their military capabilities have been wiped out.

Meanwhile, gasoline prices have risen $1 over the past month to a national average of $4/gallon, diesel is up $1.60 to $5.37/gallon, and the selloff in Treasuries over deficit and inflation concerns has increased interest rates, driving mortgage rates from just under to well over 6%. Costs for materials and other inputs are rising for businesses at the fastest pace since the energy spike that occurred when Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. The rate of inflation will rise in coming months, and the rate of economic growth will slow in a spat of stagflation that this President can’t afford in advance of a heated midterm election. This is the war that Iran is starting to win, and it has nothing to do with the battlefield.

This is why I think the President will make haste in negotiations with another optimistic announcement about negotiations on Friday, which will be followed by news that he has struck “a great deal” for all parties some time the following week. The details won’t matter if it ends the bombing, reopens the strait, and compels Israel to comply with the President’s demands. The long-term damage to the economy should be minimal, while I think investors will look past the short-term pain as temporary. Pundits more pessimistic than me point to the fact that the S&P 500 broke its long-term uptrend during this conflict on March 19, which indicates this could be the beginning of a bear market. The odds are not in their favor.

The S&P 500 has broken its 200-day moving average 153 times since 1957, and the returns that followed were not ominous at all. While the market’s performance in the month of the break is mixed, the index was higher 73% of the time three months later for an average gain of 3.5%. The win rate improves to 87% for a gain of 9.8% over the following six months. Investors saw gains 80% of the time in the 12 months that followed, averaging a return of 15.9%. A key distinction between the wins and losses was the length of time we remained below the 200-day. Short-term breaks were far more bullish, which is why the clock is ticking for President Trump in his war of words with Iran.

Volatility in markets always creates opportunities, and the one that stands out to me now is in precious metals. Gold and silver have sold off along with other risk assets, unlike what one would expect during times of geopolitical uncertainty. I attribute this to the significant outperformance for both metals since last November, which was fueled largely by new investors who viewed both metals more like meme stocks than portfolio hedges. I suspect this crowd has been washed out with bear market declines in this space over the past month, creating deeply oversold conditions.

Gold (GLD) successfully tested its 200-day moving average last week, preserving its uptrend. The same can be said for silver (SLV). These are good entry points for new or additional investment dollars.

data.title: OPKO Health: The Waiting Game Continues
data.description:
OPKO Health, Inc. (OPK) is one of my oldest positions in my portfolio. It has not been a good performer for me, but I do continue to hold the stock. The company has been going through a transition in the business. It is slimming down and putting more focus back on drug development and getting its pipeline through the FDA approval process.

The company has long-term potential due to that drug pipeline. It has a lot of good candidates and has partnered with a few different companies to help develop and bring those drugs to market. At this point in time, those drug candidates are in the early stages of the process. Nothing is likely to see much movement during 2026. I see the long-term potential but don't see a lot of movement happening in the near future.

Diagnostic Business

The company operates in two segments, one of those being the diagnostics business. The company is in the process of reducing the size of the operations in the diagnostics business. A big part of that is the sale of the BioReference business. This is a nationwide, full-service clinical laboratory. The company has sold the majority of this business and is now in the process of reducing the headcount and operations. I went into more detail on this process in previous articles. I expect this process will take a good part of 2026 to get fully cleaned up and running efficiently. They are focusing on rightsizing the ship and becoming profitable within the unit. The company completed the sale of the BioReference business during the third quarter, which means that the fourth quarter is the first full quarter since the transaction closed. Management noted on the earnings call that the post-transaction operations represented $300 million in revenue during 2025. They also stated that they expect the BioReference business will "deliver positive operating income and cash flow in 2026."

The quarter showed that the company still has some progress to make if they want to hit the goal of delivering positive operating income and cash flow. During Q4 the company reported revenue of $71.1 million for the business unit. Total costs and expenses were $89.4 million. The operating loss was $18.3 million. They still have quite a bit of improvement to go from this point. There were some one-time expenses included in there that are related to the downsizing of the business. There is also depreciation and amortization included in the figure. Regardless, the company has work to do to hit its goal in 2026.

The 4Kscore test is one of the areas that the company is confident will continue its growth going forward for the BioReference business. The 4Kscore test is a blood test that is used to detect prostate cancer. This product was FDA cleared and is still in the process of growing in the market. It had growth of 17% year over year in Q4. The big item here is the FDA approved a label change during Q3, which now does not require a digital rectal examination. This will make it much easier for primary care physicians to utilize the product. The label change should allow the company to build up some momentum with the 4Kscore test and help drive growth for the Diagnostic business.

Management provided guidance that does not show much growth, though. They estimate that Q1 will have revenue of $71-$75 million and 2026 as a whole to be $300-$312 million. This is pretty minimal growth over the $300 million figure provided for the post-acquisition results in 2025. I was hoping to see improvement on the revenue side during 2026, but if the company can reduce costs and make it profitable, then that will be a win.

Pharmaceuticals

NGENLA is an FDA-approved once-weekly growth hormone injection. The company partnered with Pfizer on this drug, and Pfizer controls the commercialization of the drug. For more information on the drug, please refer to my previous article. NGENLA looked like it was going to start to show some growth based upon results from Q4. The company recorded a gross profit share of $12.5 million during the quarter for the drug. Annualized, this would be roughly $50 million. That turns out to not be the case. The company guided for Q1 to have revenue of $5-$6 million in gross profit share. It also guided for NGENLA revenue to be $34-$37 million in 2026, only up slightly from the $32 million seen in 2025, and well below the annual run rate seen in Q4.

NGENLA at this point appears to be an underperformer. The underperformance in prior quarters and also in the prior year was supposed to be more about timing and inventory issues. The drug started to look promising in the fourth quarter, but guidance is well below that figure. It seems that investors can expect a much lower annual run rate than expected going forward. The lowest end of my estimate for the drug was $38 million, and that was for 2024. The drug is finally expected to get close to that number in 2026.

I have discussed Rayaldee extensively in my research on the stock. This drug has been a bust for the company. It has underachieved expectations, and that does not appear to be changing. The company recorded revenue of $8.8 million in Q4, down from the prior year's figure of $9.1 million. I do not expect there to be any change for Rayaldee going forward. It is really a matter of the company making sure that revenue is profitable.

The real revenue potential in this business is milestone payments over the next few years. As the company advances different drugs along the pipeline, they are eligible for milestone payments. The company is in the early stages for many of these drugs, so significant milestones are not likely in the near future but could come in 2027.

Drug Pipeline

The company still has a strong pipeline of drugs in development. I have covered these drugs more in depth in a previous article, and here covering the stock. Most of the value for OPK is in the pipeline, so it is worth a review of that pipeline and some of the potential.

A key piece of information for the drug pipeline already is that these drugs are in early stages. Three of the drugs are in Phase 1, and the rest are still preclinical. We are not likely to see anything develop from that pipeline in the near future. Progress is continuing, but approvals and monetization are still a long way out. This also means that they are not likely to drive the stock price higher in the near future either. When these drugs progress to a further stage of approval, then you will likely see some movement, but that is not a quick process.

The company has also moved into a business model of partnering on the development of their drugs and pipeline. The far-right column on the Product Pipeline slide shows the partner for each of the drugs in the pipeline. This strategy helps the company bear the cost of the drug development. The company typically collects an upfront fee, the partner helps with the costs of development and FDA approvals, and then the company gets royalties upon monetization. This strategy also provides a partner to take their drug to market. I think the company learned a lesson with the go-to-market of Rayaldee and found it valuable to have a partner with more experience in getting drugs into the market.

The biggest and most advanced drug in the pipeline is MDX2201, the vaccine for Epstein-Barr virus. This is licensed with Merck and has some large potential milestone payments and royalties. Not much has changed since the last report for this drug candidate. The drug completed phase 1 trials involving 200 adults during Q4. It is up to Merck to evaluate the results and decide on the next steps of moving to Phase 2. Phase 2 is not expected to start until the beginning of next year. So the timeline on this is still a long way out for the company.

The other two drugs in Phase 1 trials are MDX2001 and MDX2004. MDX2001 is working on moving into Phase 1b. MDX2004 is in the process of going through Phase 1 escalation. It completed the first step, and now they are looking to go to the second step. Phase 1a for that drug is expected to be completed by the end of the year. Those are still early for the company, and there is not too much to be expected in terms of revenue for the next few years. The other option, and this follows the trend for the company, is that they find a partner for those two drugs as well. OPKO would likely get a payment up front in that case. I would not be surprised to see this happen as they get more data and results around the drugs.

Regeneron is a newer partner that OPK started to work with during the fourth quarter of 2025. The companies will work together to develop multispecific antibodies. The arrangement with Regeneron has an ultimate potential of $1 billion in milestones along with double-digit royalty payments. The other part of this agreement is that Regeneron is responsible for reimbursing ModeX for its time and work in creating antibody candidates and also for funding the commercial efforts for those drugs that are selected for further development.

Financial Outlook

Guidance for losses is also much higher than I expected for the year in total. The company still has a long way to go and multiple new drugs in the market before it is going to become profitable. The company is provided guidance for revenues of $125-$140 million for Q1. The year-over-year comp is not as relevant here as the company sold off much of its BioReference business. Costs are expected to be $170-$180 million. The full year is showing a similar picture of continued losses. Revenue is expected at $530-$560 million, and expenses are estimated at $725-$750 million. That is a loss of nearly $200 million. For cash flow purposes, $100 million of that expense is depreciation and amortization.

The company has a strong cash position following the sale of the BioReference business. In all, the company received $410.8 million in proceeds from the divestiture. The company ended the year with $369 million in cash. This will provide the company plenty of cash to continue the development of its drug pipeline. The company also intends to use some of that cash to return capital to shareholders. During 2025 the company purchased 34.6 million shares for $47 million. The company has $112.7 million remaining on a share repurchase program. That is over 13% of the company's market cap. The company could get aggressive with its buybacks and help drive the stock higher.

Risks

The company is in the middle of R&D for a variety of different drugs. It is not a cheap, quick, or easy process to get a drug through the approval process. If the company cannot get approval for some of their drug pipeline, then the company will not be able to generate sufficient revenues to cover their expenses. The company will have also sunk a lot of capital into these different drugs. Biotech is a risky business that can have big payoffs. To help mitigate this risk, the company partners with other companies. It receives upfront cash and also shares expenses. This limits the upside potential but also mitigates the downside.

Guidance provided shows continued heavy losses for 2026. The company has a lot of potential drugs for the market, but those are not monetized at this point in the process. Milestone payments can come, but those are not guaranteed. The company has sufficient cash for the time being following the sale of BioReference. This could change if the company is not able to get products through the pipeline.

Conclusion

OPK continues to work on its drug pipeline. There is a lot of potential revenue within that drug pipeline, and that is where the value lies for the company. The only problem at this point is that the drug pipeline is in the very early stages. The company does not even expect to have a drug in Phase 2 by the end of the year. Monetization is still a way out for the company. I think this stagnant period will pass through to the stock price as well. The company does have a strong cash position to continue to develop this pipeline without dilution concerns.

It seems that the company is moving the BioReference business in the right direction in terms of profitability. That being said, it still has a long way to go to meet its 2026 goal of being cash flow positive in the business. Also, I was hoping to see a little more growth in the business; it seems it is going to be flat to low single-digit growth.

The stock continues to have potential for the future. At the current market cap of $850 million, the stock could turn into a long-term winner. I am just not sure if that is going to happen anytime soon. The company continues to rework the BioReference business, which will take time. It is also in the early stages of the drug pipeline. I don't see the stock moving up at the moment. The company is moving in the right direction, and I think there is long-term potential. I would look to 2027 to start to see some results from the company's efforts. The drugs will be advancing in the pipeline by then, and the diagnostics business should be looking at a year of positive returns. I will continue to hold my position and watch the stock for continued updates. I rate the stock a hold.

data.title: El Pollo Loco: Taking Bites Out Of Chipotle
data.description:
A little while ago I told you that KFC (YUM) was going through a sort of 'Taco Bell-ification.' And, by the way, El Pollo Loco (LOCO) has been Taco Bell-ifying itself for a while now.

Believe me, I missed using that term before for Pollo Loco. Oh well, some days I'm more of a 'coiner' than others.

But what really matters here is that this 'pollo' finally took off!

Last time I flagged this fast-casual as cheap, this stock already ripped ~30%. And, to tell the truth, it outperformed almost (if not all) other restaurant stocks in the same stretch.

It seems I got that right; the market moved past the 'nothing story' narrative and started pricing in a kind of asset-light turnaround.

Pollo Loco had already lined up its largest opening pipeline ever (including reused locations with top-notch IRR), improved on loyalty, and was even borrowing Taco Bell's 'Innovation Wheel.'

Narratives come and go, you might be thinking.

Fair enough. But what if they've already pulled some numbers? I mean, Q4 FY 2025 came out a few days ago, and you can already see this thesis showing up in the stock.

Right after the bell rang, the stock went up +30% and is practically sitting at my price target of $14 per share.

So, this will be quite a follow-up.

First, I want to take a peek at these numbers they pulled. Well, it matters a lot whether or not innovations are driving traffic in this soft economy.

I also think I should pull some numbers out of my head for FY 2026 and finally try to give you some idea if everything is really priced in at $14.

Just to remind you that I made it very clear that if the market truly prices this as a Chipotle-like (CMG) growth story, the EV/EBITDA would likely exceed ~11x and the upside would be much fatter.

Menu Moves That Make It Pop

To me it's clear that Pollo Loco is gaining market share from bowl-heavy fast-casuals like Chipotle as the macro worsens (especially in Q4 2025).

I remember there was even a joke about filming the Chipotle server to get them to fill your bowl.

One analyst even went around weighing bowls at different restaurants and noticed that there really was some difference between them.

And if you narrow it down to Chipotle's core customer base (mostly Millennials), you'll notice that the rejection rate exceeds 18%. In practice, this is one of the biggest drags on their same-store sales, which fell by 2.5% in the last quarter.

To me, a few things are behind that share gain.

The first one is the bowls, especially the launches (Street Corn Double Chicken and Queso Crunch Double Chicken).

Besides being cheaper than other fast-casual options, ranging from ~$12 to $15 depending on whether you want drinks or not, they are also hearty (25.7 oz.). They are even cheaper and larger than some fast-casual options like Sweetgreen (SG) and CAVA (CAVA) in standard sizes.

These Double Chicken bowls almost double the amount of protein per serving.

An Original Pollo Bowl has ~41g, these have 75 to 78g. That's ~6.25g of protein per dollar.

If you wanted that amount of protein in CAVA bowls, you'd have to order the double steak bowl and probably shell out over $20. Even then, you'd still get just under 20g less protein.

Chipotle also has a high-protein option that even surpasses Pollo Loco (~81g per serving), but people still complain about the bowl size (the average is 21.5 oz, smaller than Pollo Loco's) and it's a few dollars more expensive.

There's an opportunity for Pollo Loco in the bowl market. They just need to take what Chipotle has done wrong in recent years and do it right.

It's not rocket science.

The new salads are also following this same path. High protein content (between 40 and 65g of protein) and lower price points than traditional fast-casuals.

But there's more to it.

As I told you in a previous write-up, they are testing items like 'Loco Tenders' and some kind of chicken sandwich.

This boosts their TAM (fried chicken is the fastest-growing category in the U.S.) to compete directly with fast-food like Popeyes (QSR), KFC, Chick-fil-A, and even casual dining options like Applebee's (DIN) such as the Big Cluckin'.

Don't get me wrong. It could work if well executed, but the competition here is more brutal.

Pollo Loco historically serves flame-grilled marinated chicken, and if guests start to see it as a fast-food alternative rather than fast-casual, the average check would likely plummet.

I don't think it should become a core item or anything like that.

If they manage to push them (especially the tenders) with add-ons like sauces or similar items, that would be the best-case scenario. Back-of-house complexity would only increase slightly and perhaps even boost margins with sauces (which generally have a huge contribution margin) gaining weight.

Something more like KFC's 'Saucy.'

Numbers Don't Lie

So how does that show up in the numbers?

Well, the first thing you should pay attention to is same-store sales. Pollo Loco has outperformed some of its fast-casual/fast-food competitors and even the industry, according to the NRA.

Just take a peek here:

As you probably already know, most of Pollo Loco's company-owned restaurants (147 out of 175) are in California. And, since AB 1228, traffic there has dropped a lot.

Taking only the franchised locations (the largest chunk of locations with 328 restaurants), same-store sales grew 3.2% with a positive 0.8% increase in traffic.

These franchised restaurants are also mostly located in California, but also in Utah (10), Arizona (29), and even Texas (32). This gap seems to me to indicate that pressure is mostly coming from company-owned restaurants in California. Nothing structural.

Same-store sales and the 9 new store openings in FY 2025 pushed revenue to $123.5 million (~8% higher year-over-year, the biggest bump in at least three years) in Q4.

Best part, margins kept moving up.

If you ask me, it comes from three things:

  1. Company-owned stores are selling more (a little more, as you've seen, but it's still more), and this helps to dilute fixed costs like rent.
  2. They added four net franchised restaurants in FY 2025, and one was sold to the company (a heavier franchise mix helps margins).
  3. Pollo Loco improved costs in Food, Beverage and Packaging (70 bp lower) and Labor (90 bp lower) compared to the same period last year.

I don't know if you've noticed, but these three factors aren't one-offs and can (and probably will, based on guidance) be repeated going forward.

Doubt it? Pollo Loco should continue to drive sales in larger same-store locations (~2.4% higher as the Q1 proxy) with menu and loyalty innovations. There are 15 to 16 franchised restaurants to open in FY 2026. And commodity inflation should remain in the low single digits, which is easily offset by price increases (you can chalk that up to a chicken-heavy mix).

Looking at the New Openings (Again)

I think you're all already well aware that Pollo Loco is using failed restaurant locations to open its new locations.

That's no secret to anyone.

You should also know by now that these locations have a payback period of ~2 to 3 years, far exceeding the 4 years of Legacy locations.

But what I think I haven't told you is that they can (and should) target smaller DMAs. Including those where Chipotle doesn't yet have a very large presence.

I'll use two new locations as examples. Look at Washington and New Mexico.

Pollo Loco entered there this year and is competing with a still small Chipotle there. They have 11 locations in New Mexico (7 in Albuquerque alone) and 85 in Washington (also heavily concentrated in Seattle).

I got curious and googled that New Mexico location. It's in Albuquerque, and, judging by the reviews, they are surpassing Chipotle almost across the street.

Washington was a little different.

The Seattle store occupied a Verizon (VZ) building and was probably one of those locations that only cost $1 million or a little more.

It didn't look like a Verizon store. It had something that looked like a drive-thru.

I dug a little deeper and discovered that it had previously been Schlotzsky's.

I told you all this to show that they don't necessarily need to scoop up buildings that were restaurants.

A gas station and a Verizon store. That seems like a lot of variety to me.

So, with lower construction costs and consequently stronger unit-level economies, I see them building some kind of cluster strategy in capitals with smaller DMAs, and then they could even go to small towns and suburbs.

Still a 'Buy' Post-Run?

Management gave us strong guidance.

Regarding what matters most, same-store sales should be between 2% and 3%. They will also open 3 to 4 company-owned restaurants and 15 to 16 franchised restaurants.

However, they didn't give any guidance on revenue. I'll borrow the Wall St. projection of $497.7 million.

Adjusted EBITDA would be $66 to $68 million. An adjusted EBITDA margin of ~13.4% at the midpoint. Slightly above the $66.7 million they projected for the entire FY 2025.

And I still think that's a bit conservative.

If I use something close to 13% for unadjusted EBITDA, that would be just under $65 million for FY 2026.

I'm also projecting an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of ~80% (average of the last two years), so OCF would come in at $52 million. As they are projecting Capex of $37 to $40 million, FCF would come in at something close to $13.5 million.

Taking that FCF and adding it to cash to reduce long-term net debt, it would likely sit at $31.3 million.

So I've got a pretty clear setup for FY 2026.

Management even added some color to FY 2027 and FY 2028. Sales growing in the mid-single digits and EBITDA in the high single digits.

It's not too far-fetched to imagine revenue of $522.5 million for FY 2027 and $548.6 million for FY 2028. For EBITDA, something between $70.2 million in FY 2027 and $75.8 million in FY 2028.

Using the numbers I mentioned before, Pollo Loco is trading at ~10.1x EBITDA.

Even if I used the same 5-year average multiples (11.5x EBITDA) that I used in the last write-up, I would arrive at a price of just over $17 per share for FY 2026.

That's already enough to project an upside of almost 19% from here.

But would Pollo Loco really trade at a multiple of 11.5x even if it has some kind of asset-light turnaround?

Well, I would say no.

This chain has been weak for much of the last 5 years, and even so, they managed an average of 11.5x. So, if we aim for something close to 15x for FY 2028 and already discount 9.6%, I would find something close to $22.80.

An upside of almost 60% in two years. Not bad, if you ask me.

Triangulating with a Graham Model (EPS of $0.97 in FY 2026 growing to $1.28 for FY 2028, based on 30 million shares and a net margin of ~7%), I found a price target of $19.15 already with a 25% haircut.

That said, I'll keep the 'Buy' here.

I'll use the more conservative price target ($17 per share) in the short term, but I'm still aiming for the top end in the medium/long term.

data.title: Acuity: Bring On The Q2 Earnings Call
data.description:
Previous Coverage

I first covered Acuity Inc. (AYI) in June of last year, a little after the release of the company's Q2 2025 earnings report, which was not well-received by the market, as the stock declined more than 10% following that call. Since my initial write-up, the stock has fluctuated a lot but ultimately climbed about 6%, trailing the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust by about 300 bps. Prior to the last earnings report in early January, the stock had seen strong price appreciation of almost 40%, but softer 2026 guidance and a lack of organic revenue growth and negative market sentiment led to the stock price tumbling.

With Acuity set to release their Q2 2026 earnings report in about a week, I figured now would be a good time to re-evaluate the stock. We'll take a look at the Q1 2026 earnings call, see what Q2 might bring, and provide an updated valuation based on revised free cash flow figures.

Q1 2026 Earnings & Q2 Projections

As mentioned above, on Thursday, January 8th, Acuity announced their Q1 2026 earnings results with non-GAAP earnings per share of $4.69, topping analyst expectations by $0.10, on revenue of $1.14B, matching the consensus but surging almost 20% year-over-year. Additionally, GAAP earnings per share also came in $0.13 above estimates, at $3.82.

Let's start with the Acuity Brands Lighting segment first, which earned revenue of $895.1M and made up about ~80% of total sales. Relative to Q1 2025, total sales ticked up 1%, or about $9M, with the Independent Sales Network contributing the most to that increase, rising about 3%, or slightly more than $20M YoY. Direct Sales nearly negated the gains by IS, as revenue sank about $17M compared to 25Q1. It's worth noting, Direct sales is known to be less predictable and somewhat susceptible to DoT or Municipal spending, so this is nothing new but can lead to significant swings. And lastly, the Corporate division posted a healthy year-over-year jump, with sales increasing about $7M.

ABL's gross profit dipped from $406.4M to $400.6M, a decline of about 1.5%, as rising production costs more than offset gains made due to lower input costs and higher revenue. These results equated to a GPM of 44.8%, a contraction of 110 bps relative to Q1 2025. Conversely, the operating margin expanded 40 bps to close out the quarter at 16.6%, with the operating income rising a little more than $5M, driven by a small decline in employee wages.

Moving on to the Intelligent Spaces segment, where the QSC acquisition directly led to strong sales growth of $184M, or more than 250%. Although management did not specifically state how much growth came from legacy products versus the QSC deal; we can estimate, based on a Q1 2025 revenue figure of $73.5M (which did not include QSC) and management stating revenue increased in the mid-teens, that the legacy business contributed approximately $80M to $95M, leaving QSC to make up the remaining roughly ~$170M.

The gross profit also benefitted from the QSC deal as it rose more than $110M, while the GPM earned decent growth, expanding by about 100 bps relative to the same quarter a year ago. The operating margin contracted minimally to close out the quarter at 14.4%, with the operating profit seeing similar growth as revenue and GPM numbers.

As previously discussed, market sentiment following the earnings release, which can be read/listened to here, was negative as shares dropped as much as 15% in midday trading, eventually closing down nearly 13%. Similarly, over the past month shares of AYI have continued to retreat as the stock is down a little more than 6% during that time.

Lastly, the company's Q2 earnings will come out next Thursday, April 2nd, with analysts estimating normalized EPS of $4.06, equating to YoY growth of about 9%, and revenue forecasted to grow almost 8% and come in at $1.09B.

Risks/Headwinds

Acuity has had a rough start to 2026, and things may not get easier if the company is unable to address some of their important headwinds.

An obvious risk facing Acuity is the expected and continued synergies stemming from the QSC acquisition. Although the company has already seen the benefits of this deal, they must continue executing, as integrating a new business rarely goes as planned. The AIS segment approximately tripled in size following the deal, with segment sales jumping from around 7% to more than 20%, so even a small setback could spook investors.

Another issue relates to potentially softer demand in the Independent Sales network, which management has stated they saw evidence of in the second half of FY25. This occurred as customers increased their inventory to combat potential tariff uncertainty and annual price increases. It's expected this segment could see a contraction in the gross profit margin due to this short-term headwind.

Acuity Valuation & Future Return Potential

The final piece of this puzzle is to determine if AYI is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so, we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield and specifically how the current yield compares to its historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have typically been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks today relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data, capturing the rolling five-year average free cash flow yield for the past seven years. The valuation chart below juxtaposes AYI's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The key assumption behind this valuation model is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past five years, on average.

But since every valuation is an estimate, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates Acuity is trading for a small premium, as the fair value suggested is $267, and the stock sits close to $285. The last time this stock traded close to its fair value range was in April of last year, and up until recently it was on quite the run. Acuity boasts a decent forward return potential of 8.16%, with the pieces of that estimate being a forward dividend yield of 0.28%, a return to fair value factor of -1.36%, and a solid earnings growth rate just north of 9%.

Final Thoughts

In summary, Acuity has seen its ups and downs since my initial coverage but has ultimately come up a little short of the broad market. The Q1 earnings report was solid, but I believe because management did not raise fiscal 2026 guidance during the call, that's what likely led to negative market sentiment and the subsequent sell-off. The stock appears to be trading close to fair value right now with a decent return potential, although slightly below my typical 10% threshold. Ultimately, there are a lot of unknowns heading into this earnings release, and because of that, I think rating the stock a HOLD is appropriate until another earnings call can be digested.

data.title: BrainsWay: Strong Execution, But Valuation Limits Upside
data.description:
BrainsWay Ltd. (BWAY)has been on a winning streak since I reviewed the stock in August. I rated it a speculative buy on price pullbacks. The company was showing good growth and had good margins. I considered the valuation more than I wanted to pay at the time for the company and was looking to enter a position on any price pullback. That never materialized and I never entered a position. I missed the opportunity as the stock has increased nearly 67% since that time.

The price has increased as the company has executed well. The company is evolving from a company bringing their technology to market to a growing annual recurring revenue source company. The leasing model has continued to expand, growing revenues and also providing a consistent revenue source. Revenue estimates have been raised along with EBITDA estimates. There is backing for the runup in the stock price. I think the run up has put the price at a point where there is limited upside potential.

Financials

The company continued the strong growth trend. It also continued to show good margins and profitability. The company reported revenue of $14.5 million in Q4, an increase of 27% from the prior year. Full year showed the same growth of 27% from the prior year, with revenues reaching $52.2 million. The gross margin continued to be a strength as well, coming in at 75.4% for the year, up from 74.6% in the prior year.

The company also continued to improve the profit profile. The company continues to scale and the leverage is showing on the financials. Operating expenses continue to become a smaller percentage of revenue. Operating expenses made up 67.1% of revenue in 2025 versus 71.2% in 2024. This leverage passed through to the profit line for the company. It reported operating income of $1.9 million in Q4, up from $0.4 million the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA increased 53% to $2.3 million. For the year the company saw operating income of $4.3 million for the year, up from $1.4 million in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was $7 million, compared to $4.5 million in 2024.

The company also ended the year with a strong balance sheet with $68 million in cash and no debt. The company is already operating at a profit so this cash position will only grow or it could be used for acquisitions, or as will be reviewed later, strategic investments.

The company has provided guidance that maintains that strong growth and increased profits. It provided guidance for 2026 revenues to reach $66-$68 million, representing growth of 27%-30%. It also expects operating income of 13%-14% and adjusted EBITDA of $12-$14 million. On the high end the adjusted EBITDA number would be double the 2025 figure. This shows the profit potential as the company continues to scale. The licensing model has continued to grow and be very profitable for the company. This provides consistent revenues that are high margin as well.

Overall the financial profile continues to be strong for the company. It was solid when I reviewed it in August and it has continued to grow and generate cash.

Leasing Model and ARR

One of things that has driven the business the most is the leasing model. The company has seen strong growth with leases to customers rather than the customer purchasing the machine outright. This allows companies to avoid the upfront cost of the machine. It also allows them to charge the lease off as an expense on their financials, and also it means that BrainsWay will manage maintenance on the equipment. These are some important factors, particularly with larger enterprises who manage more locations.

The leasing model provides the company with a steady recurring revenue source. The stability in revenues cannot be understated, to know that you are going to get the same revenue each month through the lease program provides a guide for management to more efficiently run the company. They can plan and optimize costs much more efficiently with stability. The leases also have high rates of customer renewals at 93% customer retention rate. Once the machine is in the location and generating returns it is much easier for the customer to continue the lease. The lease agreements also generate a higher gross margin. So as the company continues to drive more lease agreements they should see an uptick in the gross margin.

The company ended the year with $70 million in remaining performance obligations. This leaves a strong and growing revenue pipeline.

Developments and Catalysts

The company expects to continue its growth through a few different sources: organic growth, strategic investments, new care settings/territories, and new indications.

Most recently the company received FDA clearance for Accelerated (SWIFT) Deep TMS Protocol for MDD in September 2025. This will expand the addressable patient population for the company. The company also noted during the earnings call that they have added coverage from another insurance company for accelerated Deep TMS. The company will look to continue to expand coverage for this procedure following the FDA approval. The SWIFT treatment is important due to the shorter time window for treatment. One of the issues with going to market with Deep TMS has been the large number of required visits. The SWIFT treatment is less visits and much less invasive for the patient. The company hopes this will drive additional patients and revenue.

Another potential future use the company is working with is PTSD. The company is in the process of preparing a submission to the FDA for treatment of PTSD with the VA system. This is already occurring in Israel. The Israeli Ministry of Defense Rehabilitation Department approved reimbursement for Deep TMS therapy for PTSD. This is a large field and much needed support for patients. It is something to watch for FDA approval in the US as it could be a future revenue driver. The other two indications that the company is looking into are for Alcohol Use Disorder and Alzheimer. These are further out future potential.

The company has continued to make strategic investments in mental health providers. These investments provide capital to providers which allows them to expand and grow. As the providers expand and grow the use of BrainsWay products will grow as well. At this point the company has made investments into 5 mental health networks. The company has seen success with this strategy so far and expects to continue to invest in these providers. Management noted they have identified up to 200 qualified clinics as potential candidates for investment. The company wants to be able to utilize the data they can gather from these different locations to improve their technology and continue to innovate within the mental health space. So far this strategy has been successful for the company, but it does add a layer of risk. They are investing in companies that they do not control. If those companies fail then the investment made by BrainsWay is lost.

Valuation

The company has been performing well and has a lot of future potential opportunities to continue strong growth. The ARR coming in allows the company to get pretty accurate estimates going forward. I am sure the way the company is executing that it will hit the estimates provided.

The stock has had a strong run up over the past year. This has left the stock trading at a premium. The company trades at a PE of 74.5 and PS of 9.8. It has a forward PE of 44 for 2026 and 30 for 2027. The forward PS comes in at 8 for 2026 and 6.6 for 2027. These are pretty high forward valuations. The company is growing and that is expected to continue with revenue growth of 27% estimated for 2026 and 22% for 2027. Management also provided an estimate for adjusted EBITDA of $12 to $14 million. That would leave a forward multiple of 38.3-44.6 on adjusted EBITDA.

The company does have a cash position of $68 million as well to take into consideration for the valuation. Even with this the valuation is a high premium.

Risks

The company is developing and bringing to market a new technology. The adoption of this technology is not guaranteed. The product must be able to prove its value within the marketplace and by users. The company does have FDA approval and its product is being adopted. This should help provide confidence for customers. It is still considered new technology and may seem unproven in the market. I also wonder how many healthcare locations would be looking to adopt such a technology at this point in time. It might take some time to continue to develop the market and prove the technology as valuable for patients.

The strategic investments into healthcare providers also brings in an investment risk for the company. It is investing money in these businesses in the hope that they will grow. That growth will benefit BrainWays as they are users of the technology. The company does not have control or oversight of these companies but is merely an investor. Any one or all of the investments may not materialize.

Conclusion

BrainsWay has executed well over the past year. It has continued to grow revenues at a steady rate and improve its profitability. It has capitalized on the leasing model and ARR. This provides steady long term cash flow for the business.

I previously rated it a speculative buy and never found the opportunity to purchase shares. I missed out on the run the stock has seen since that time. The stock price increase has left the stock at a premium valuation. The company trades at high PS, PE, and EBITDA multiples.

While I like what the company has done and think it has room to continue to grow its revenues going forward, I am hesitant to buy at the current price. I think at the current valuations the growth is already priced into the stock. I rate it a hold.

data.title: Kuaishou: Challenging Prospects Overshadow Q4 Outperformance (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've turned 'Neutral' on Kuaishou Technology (KUASF) (1024.HK).

It's fair to have the stock as a hold-rated name. There are positives such as the above-consensus 4Q numbers and the AI video division's strong momentum. But I can't ignore negatives like the anticipated revenue slowdown and margin compression for the near term.

The prior November 24, 2025 update highlighted my constructive view of its marketing/AI segments and third-quarter results.

Topline And Operating Profitability Beat Consensus

KUASF's latest financials were revealed in a press release issued on the morning of March 25.

Its turnover and "Operating Profit/OP" were 12% and 36% higher YoY at CNY39.6B and CNY5.8B, respectively, for Oct-Dec 2025.

I'm encouraged by the group's better-than-anticipated showing. Analysts had previously projected a weaker +9% growth (source: S&P Capital IQ). KUASF's 4Q25 OP-to-revenue of 14.6% exceeded the sell-side's earlier prediction by 48 bps.

I wrote in the late-Nov '25 article that the "Online Marketing Services/OMS" business is "benefiting from increased AI utilization in many ways." Its "generative recommendation large model OneRec" and "fully-automated media placement tool Universal Auto X/UAX" were perceived to be catalysts in my opinion. I was proven to be right.

This division's recent three-month sales of CNY23,618M represented a +15% year-on-year rise. The 4Q25 share of advertising spend unrelated to e-commerce captured by UAX was slightly under 80 percent. That's a meaningful improvement from 3Q25's 70+%. Also, KUASF disclosed at the results briefing that OneRec was responsible for "5% of growth" in OMS's quarterly top line.

Separately, the firm had become smarter about how to enlarge the user base. It enjoyed fixed-cost leverage effects too.

The "Selling & Marketing" or "S&M" expenses-to-revenue metric improved by 3 ppts from 32% in 4Q2024 to 29% for 4Q2025. KUASF indicated in its earnings announcement that the company optimized "acquisition strategies across channels." One example shared was the use of "AI technology to enhance push strategies" which lifted the "user open rate."

Also, its OpEx-to-sales ratio fell 250 bps to 42% in the final quarter of last year. I see this as a good illustration of how the enterprise is scaling profitably by spreading its non-variable costs.

But Adverse Outlook Warrants Share Pullback

Its stock price experienced a -8.7% drop on Wednesday. My take is that the firm's guidance had hurt investor confidence.

Management estimated at the Q4 meeting that "Capital Expenditures/CAPEX" will surge 73% to CNY26B for the current year.

KUASF mentioned in the results release that it is "progressing steadily with the construction of a new computer center." This is consistent with the group's call commentary regarding "reserving expansion space to accommodate long-term needs" pertaining to "server and bandwidth."

I can understand why the enterprise needs to invest in infrastructure to support future growth. It's unavoidable that short-term profitability will be sacrificed. My forecasts point to KUASF's operating margin contracting by 3 ppts to 11.5% this year.

KUASF also shared with analysts its expectations of a "1Q26 revenue growth of 2.5%." That's much slower than 1Q2025's and 4Q2025's increases of +10.9% and +11.8%, respectively.

I've identified two factors contributing to this deceleration.

Bloomberg reported in late-2025 that Chinese authorities have taken actions to "crack down on tax evasion by merchants who use the online platforms." This means some OMS clients have less money to spend on ads because of increased fiscal obligations.

Also, SA News piece published last month drew attention to the "cutthroat rivalry" among domestic "internet platform companies" in the PRC. You can imagine that KUASF's e-commerce unit will have to remain competitive by providing monetary incentives or fee waivers to its sellers.

Considering the above-mentioned, I think it's likely the group's turnover growth will moderate from a low-teens percent for FY25 to a mid-single digit in FY26.

Mixed Thoughts About Kling

I touched on the "long runway" for "KUASF's AI video creation solution called Kling AI/KAI" in my late-Nov 2025 write-up.

KAI's "Annualized Recurring Revenue/ARR" jumped by more than 40% sequentially from Dec 2025's $0.214B to Jan 2026's $0.30B+. At the analyst call, management emphasized that "we are confident that Kling AI's revenue in 2026 will more than double."

I concur with KUASF's bullishness about this space. "The global market for AI video generation is expected to grow at a 19.9% CAGR from 2024-2030" based on research cited by contributor "Michael Del Monte."

On the flip side, KAI will have to remain loss-making for a prolonged period of time, if the firm wants to avoid ceding ground to competitors.

SA News mentioned in Feb that "Seedance 2.0, an AI video generator from TikTok owner ByteDance (BDNCE), is already creating some buzz." In response, KUASF talked about "major Kling AI model upgrades" at the results meeting. These initiatives form part of the company's guided CNY11B additional CAPEX commitment this year.

My Closing Remarks

I'm changing KUASF's rating from 'Buy' to 'Hold.' I've taken into account KAI's long-term potential and the below-expectations management outlook.

Similar to my Nov 2025 update, I value this name using a "fair 1x PEG or Price-to-Earnings Growth."

The forward P/E and the consensus FY25-28 net profit CAGR for KUASF are 8.9x and +7.9%, respectively. The derived PEG of 1.12 times (8.9/7.9), which is close to 1x, suggests that the stock is reasonably priced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cheesecake Factory: Comp Sales Decay And Margin Erosion (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The stock market has been stubbornly bearish this year, as investors have juggled a plethora of negative headlines ranging from AI disruption, war in Iran, and a persistently shaky U.S. macroeconomy. Amid this backdrop, I continue to advocate for careful stock selection as the best way to beat the stock market: picking up shares of opportunistically declining value stocks while divesting out of recent outperformers.

Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), the restaurant operator behind some of the largest-format restaurant chains in the U.S., has managed to defy macro weakness this year, with its stock up ~7% since January and ~10% over the trailing twelve months. The question for investors now is: can Cheesecake Factory keep defying the bearish market and continue rallying, or is now a good time to lock in gains on this position?

I last wrote a buy article on Cheesecake in October, when the stock was trading in the low $50s. Since then, Cheesecake has been one of the few stocks in my portfolio to see meaningful gains. At the same time, however, I also think fundamental trends are decaying for the company, given the recent erosion in comp sales that is a reflection of weaker consumer spending. I see an opportunity for profit-taking, and am cutting my rating on this stock to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for the company. On the bright side for Cheesecake:

  • Market-topping, huge AUVs with opportunity for carryout orders. The average Cheesecake Factory generates $12.3 million in annual sales. It's relatively unique among large, big-box restaurants to drive this scale of volume. It is also boosting sales via an added focus on carryout (UberEats and DoorDash) orders.
  • Large untapped opportunity for expansion. The company's footprint is still relatively small, with only 216 owned locations in North America and 35 locations licensed to partners overseas. The company believes it has opportunity for 300 locations domestically, with substantial untapped opportunity overseas.

At the same time, however, we are wary of the following drivers:

  • Little differentiation and a very crowded restaurant industry. Cheesecake Factory and its portfolio of brands (which include North Italia, Flower Child, and a number of smaller brands aren't particularly known or valued for a particular cuisine. We're entering into a very choppy macro environment in which consumers may be more choosy about how they spend their money on going out.
  • Margin compression. The company's adjusted EBITDA has fallen recently, and amid inflation in many core categories like beef and seafood, the company may see pressure in maintaining its margin profile in FY26.

Of course, we do note that Cheesecake retains a relatively cheap valuation. The stock's 14x forward P/E is well below many peers (I consider Darden (DRI), the parent of Olive Garden and LongHorn Steakhouse, as the closest pure-play peer to Cheesecake, though its premium is slightly justified by ~4% comp sales growth whereas Cheesecake's comps are flattish).

This discount is what keeps me from being overly bearish on Cheesecake, despite the recent erosion in performance. That said, given the stock's rise since the start of the year while many high-quality businesses (particularly in the software business) have been crushed, I think it's also a good time to lock in some gains here to redeploy capital into more opportunistic value plays.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Cheesecake's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Cheesecake's revenue grew 4.4% y/y to $961.6 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $948.9 million (+3.0% y/y) by one-point margin, while also largely keeping pace against 4.8% y/y growth in Q3. However, there's an important note to keep in mind here: in Q4, the company recognized $17.3 million of gift card breakage revenue, which constituted 1.8% of the quarter's sales. As the term implies, gift card breakage results from the company determining that a certain portion of the company's outstanding gift card liabilities aren't likely to be redeemed, due primarily to age. Needless to say, while gift card breakage is a "normal" source of revenue that is trued-up from time to time, it's not an underlying performance indicator, and without this benefit Cheesecake's y/y revenue growth rates would have cut in half.

To me, the more concerning indicators are around comp sales growth (which as a reminder measures the revenue growth from existing restaurants, and excludes the contribution of newly opened restaurants as well as any impact from gift card breakage).

As shown above, comp sales in the core Cheesecake Factory brand (which was $681 million, or 71% of overall sales in the quarter) declined -2.2% y/y, worsening 250bps from 0.3% growth in Q3. Also, North Italia declined -4%, also worsening from -3% in Q3.

The company mainly blamed industry declines for the weaker performance, but it should be more accurately attributed to the company lapping the benefits of menu price increases last year while traffic continued to decline. On the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call, the company called out 3.5-4.0% of pricing benefit offset by -180bps of mix (guests selecting cheaper menu items to compensate for higher prices), which implies traffic declined by -3.9% to -4.4%.

Per CFO Matthew Clark's comments on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call:

Sure, Jim, this is Matt. So Q4 pricing was about 3.5% to 4% and mix was a negative 1.8% and then traffic was the delta from that. So as anticipated, we still did see some negative mix. I would say, though, that's a full quarter number, and we had just rolled out the new menu items really ending in early September. So we didn't have a lot of traction if we think about our average guest comes once a quarter.

So as I noted, I think just importantly, what we saw in December and now again in January is an actual improvement year-over-year in incident rates. So some of the pricing investment will be offset by increased ordering. So we do think that the negative mix will continue, but at a lesser rate as we progress through the year. Importantly, too, as we look at January, when you look at alcohol plus nonalcoholic beverages, it was almost a breakeven on incident rate.

So I think it's really guests are coming in and getting that full Cheesecake experience. So I don't know, we're kind of saying, if you think about the guide, probably a negative 1% for the year on a mix perspective based on continuing to roll out the bowls and the bites, but getting some positive on the order rate."

I have two main concerns here: first, implied traffic deceelrated further from the roughly -2.5% decline in Q3; second, peers are holding up better. As shown below, Darden showcased positive comps in all of its brands, with Olive Garden growing 3.2% y/y and LongHorn SteakHouse growing 7.2% y/y.

Yes, the macro environment may be unfavorable for restaurants and dining out right now: but Cheesecake Factory appears to be getting disproportionately hit.

Meanwhile, Cheesecake's profit margins are also eroding. Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $93.8 million declined -3% y/y, hitting a 9.8% margin that declined -70bps y/y versus 10.5% in the year-ago Q4.

The company is expecting low single digit inflation in its food basket for Q1, which may translate across the year as well; and arguably Cheesecake has limited room to keep pulling up prices without alienating customers and dragging traffic down further.

Key takeaways

In my view, investors now have an excellent opportunity to lock in gains on Cheesecake Factory while the stock is rebounding, despite the slide in comp sales that bodes ill for the start of FY26. With adjusted EBITDA falling and inflation expected to continue eating into margins this year, it's tough to see Cheesecake meaningfully catalyzing an upward re-rating in its valuation multiples, however cheap the stock is today. Begin to trim the position here.

data.title: I'm Less Positive On H.B. Fuller After This Quarter (Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This is the third time I’ve written about H.B. Fuller Company (FUL), which is a global company that makes adhesives used in many industries, including packaging, hygiene products, electronics, and construction. In my most recent analysis, I rated the stock as a buy. I did this because the company was selling off parts of its business that made lower profits, focusing more on higher-value adhesive products, and beginning to show signs of improving profit margins and stronger cash flow.

Since my last two calls, the stock tried to break out, fell back, and ended up roughly flat, more or less tracking the broader market (SP500). Today, after going through its latest earnings report, I’m switching my take on the shares to a neutral stance because the margin story is holding up better than demand.

Making Sense of H.B. Fuller’s Q1 2026

  • Net revenue: $770.8M (−2.3% YoY)
  • Organic revenue growth: −6.6% YoY
  • Price impact: +0.6%
  • Volume impact: −7.2%
  • FX impact: +3.6%
  • M&A impact: +0.7%

Let’s start with why sales were weak. The issue wasn’t pricing. The company didn’t suddenly start discounting or lose its ability to charge what it normally charges. In fact, they kept pricing fairly disciplined. They even pushed through some price increases. On top of that, they got a small lift from currency movements and from a small acquisition they did.

But despite all that, sales still slipped. The real issue was volume. Basically, customers buying less adhesive. And that’s important because adhesives like the ones H.B. Fuller sells go into a ton of industries: packaging, construction materials, electronics, hygiene products, industrial manufacturing, things like that. Management essentially said the environment is “challenging.”

Customers are still tiptoeing around new orders. Especially in areas tied to construction and general industrial activity. At the same time, input costs (the costs of materials and supplies used to make products) have started to increase again.

Reuters reported this month that manufacturing in the United States was, technically, still growing, but they back up the previous point that the cost of inputs was rising more quickly, mainly because of tariffs and higher metals prices. At the same time, economic growth in the euro zone was close to stalling, as delivery times became longer and inflation pressures increased, partly due to the current mess in the Middle East.

  • Gross margin: 30.6%
  • Adjusted gross margin: 31.3% (+170 bps YoY)
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $118.7M (+4% YoY)
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin: 15.4% (+90 bps YoY)

The good thing is that profit margins improved anyway. Management said this happened because of cost savings from restructuring, changes in the company’s business portfolio, the mix of acquisitions, and pricing that kept up with raw material costs. That's credible. But it also has limits. The key question is whether the improvement comes from long-term structural changes or from temporary internal actions taken by the company. In this quarter, the improvement in margins appears to be genuine. Even so, it’s hard for me to see this lasting without stable customer demand.

  • SG&A: $184.5M
  • Adjusted SG&A: $175.7M

Operating expenses add another factor to consider. At first glance, it looks like expenses increased. However, the company explained that if you adjust the numbers to remove the effects of currency exchange changes and the costs from newly acquired businesses, the company’s core spending was actually kept under control. Management seems to be trying to avoid a very common mistake: continuing to spend aggressively on growth projects during a period when customer demand is weak. Showing restraint in spending during a slow market is a positive sign and deserves some credit.

  • Net income: $21.0M
  • Adjusted net income: $31.5M
  • Reported EPS (diluted): $0.38
  • Adjusted EPS (diluted): $0.57

At the same time, there's still a noticeable difference between the company’s reported earnings and its adjusted earnings. The gap's still the price of restructuring, organizational changes, and system upgrades working their way through the P&L.

Segment Revenue

  • Hygiene, Health & Consumable Adhesives: $346.5M
  • Engineering Adhesives: $242.4M
  • Building Adhesive Solutions: $181.9M

Segment Adjusted EBITDA

  • Hygiene, Health & Consumable Adhesives: $48.0M
  • Engineering Adhesives: $48.2M
  • Building Adhesive Solutions: $21.6M

Segment EBITDA Margin

  • Hygiene, Health & Consumable Adhesives: 13.9%
  • Engineering Adhesives: 19.9%
  • Building Adhesive Solutions: 11.9%

Meanwhile, the mix of business segments also gives us an important insight. Engineering Adhesives once again appears to be the strongest business in the company’s portfolio. It converts more of its sales into profit and has a stronger position in its markets compared with the other segments. The result also matches what’s happening across the market, where higher-value, specialized adhesives tend to perform better and hold stronger margins.

Demand tied to electronics and specialized applications is still holding up better than demand for lower-value, more commodity-like adhesives. Which is exactly what the recent market news has been telling us. Reuters reported this week that semiconductor manufacturing and advanced chip packaging activity in China remains strong. Because H.B. Fuller has greater exposure to these higher-tech, specialized areas, its products are used more in markets that are still performing well.

By comparison, the Hygiene, Health and Consumable Adhesives side of the business showed weaker sales volumes. Once again, it likely means customers in consumer markets are still ordering cautiously, sticking to what they need instead of stocking up. In the meantime, the Building Adhesive Solutions segment looked stable in terms of profitability. Even so, it's not showing much growth. The explanation is the same as the broader market. Private construction's softened, uncertainty remains, and customers are slow to commit to new work.

  • Total debt: $2.076B
  • Cash: $107.9M
  • Net debt: $1.968B
  • Net debt / EBITDA: 3.1×
  • Net working capital: $585.9M
  • Net working capital / annualized revenue: 19.0%

Cash flow and the company’s balance sheet looked reasonably solid. But not perfect. In addition, the company’s leverage is clearly lower than it was a year ago. That helps.
